title: Grafana Image Renderer Timeout and Failure Rate
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.6.1 interactive dashboard load analytics, UC-13.6.2 datasource query error analytics, UC-13.6.3 unified alerting scheduler analytics, UC-13.6.4 generic plugin skew analytics, UC-13.6.5 authentication auditing, UC-13.6.6 annotation storms, UC-13.6.7 dashboard version revert detection, UC-13.6.8 API token lifecycle monitoring, UC-13.6.10 permission audits, UC-13.6.11 LDAP synchronization, UC-13.6.13 notification delivery failures, or deep query-text optimization for each datasource type beyond Performance host pressure proxies.
evidence: Saved search observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate with fifteen minute schedule; observability index dashboards drilling into renderer sourcetypes; alert routing to platform and BI on-call; weekly CSV exports archived to a restricted evidence index.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab namespace, throttle CPU or shrink /dev/shm on a Grafana Image Renderer pod, trigger PDF exports for an allow-listed dashboard with many panels, and confirm observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate returns HIGH or CRITICAL with non-null recommended_action within thirty minutes.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After restoring healthy CPU, memory, and shared memory, with quiet synthetic probes and steady datasource Performance hosts, confirm severities return to LOW and the alert wrapper emits zero rows when filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH only for thirty consecutive runs.

dataSources: `index=observability` `sourcetype=grafana:rendering` for Grafana Image Renderer service logs; `sourcetype=grafana:log` for Grafana core lines referencing the image renderer plugin, headless Chromium stderr, navigation failures, and export timings; `index=k8s_metrics` with `mstats` on cadvisor style `container_cpu_usage_seconds_total` and `container_memory_working_set_bytes` for renderer pods; NetData or cAdvisor `/metrics` forwarded via OpenTelemetry when present; `index=synthetic` Splunk Synthetic Monitoring browser checks for HTTPS render URLs; `index=grafana_audit` or observability `sourcetype=grafana:audit` for scheduled report and render lifecycle events; accelerated CIM `Performance` for datasource host CPU pressure correlates upstream RED signals; optional `index=metrics` Prometheus scrape lines for Grafana query duration when mirrored from datasource proxies.
app: Splunk Add-on for OpenTelemetry (`splunk-opentelemetry`), Splunk Add-on for Kubernetes (`splunk-app-for-infrastructure`), custom Grafana log forwarders, Splunk Synthetic Monitoring, optional Splunk Observability Cloud Add-on, plus Universal Forwarder or HTTP Event Collector patterns aligned with Grafana Image Renderer deployments.
spl:
`comment("UC-13.6.9 Grafana image renderer timeout failure rate multisearch renderer logs k8s_metrics synthetic tstats Performance audit join grafana_renderer_inventory earliest=-24h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=observability (sourcetype="grafana:rendering" OR (sourcetype="grafana:log" (renderer OR "image-renderer" OR rendering OR chromium OR headless))) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval lane="renderer_core_log"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(render_id, correlation_id, request_id, trace_id, x_request_id, renderId, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, dashboardUid, uid, dash_uid, "")))
      | eval datasource_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(datasource_uid, datasourceUid, ds_uid, "")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pod_name, pod, k8s_pod_name, kube_pod, ""))))
      | eval render_ms=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(render_duration_ms, duration_ms, elapsed_ms, "0"))), 10)
      | eval http_st=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(http_status, status_code, "200"))), 10)
      | eval fail=if(http_st>=500 OR match(lr,"timeout|timed out|navigation failed|chrome crashed|oomkilled|killed process|context deadline|econnreset"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=if(match(lr,"scheduled report|reporting|pdf|png export|render_key|report job"), 1, 0)
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=1
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
    [ | mstats avg(_value) AS metric_val WHERE index=k8s_metrics earliest=-24h@h latest=now span=5m (metric_name="container_cpu_usage_seconds_total" OR metric_name="container_memory_working_set_bytes") BY pod, metric_name
      | eval lane="k8s_mstats_renderer"
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(pod)))
      | where like(pod_name,"%render%") OR like(pod_name,"%image%") OR like(pod_name,"%grafana%")
      | eval org_id="0"
      | eval dashboard_uid="k8s_metrics_join"
      | eval render_id="mstats_".pod_name."_".replace(metric_name, ".", "_")
      | eval render_ms=if(metric_name=="container_cpu_usage_seconds_total", round(metric_val*1000,2), round(metric_val/1048576,4))
      | eval fail=if(metric_name=="container_memory_working_set_bytes" AND metric_val>1073741824 AND like(pod_name,"%render%"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=0
      | eval ctr_for_rate=0
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=round(metric_val,6) ]
    [ search index=synthetic sourcetype=synthetic:browser earliest=-24h@h latest=now (url=*render* OR url=*grafana*)
      | eval lane="synthetic_browser_render"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(check_id, test_id, run_id, correlation_id, synthetic_id, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, dashboardUid, target_uid, "synthetic_board")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name="synthetic_probe"
      | eval render_ms=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(duration_ms, response_time_ms, ttfb_ms, "0"))), 10)
      | eval http_st=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(http_status, status_code, "200"))), 10)
      | eval fail=if(http_st>=500 OR match(lr,"timeout|error|failed navigation"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=1
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
    [ | tstats summariesonly=t perc95(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS ds_latency_proxy FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
      | rename Performance.host AS perf_host
      | eval lane="tstats_perf_upstream"
      | eval perf_host=lower(trim(toString(perf_host)))
      | eval dashboard_uid="upstream_datasource_pressure"
      | eval org_id="0"
      | eval pod_name=perf_host
      | eval render_id="perf_".perf_host
      | eval render_ms=0
      | eval fail=if(ds_latency_proxy>92, 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=0
      | eval ctr_for_rate=0
      | eval datasource_uid=perf_host ]
    [ search (index=grafana_audit OR index=observability) sourcetype="grafana:audit" earliest=-24h@h latest=now (report OR render OR pdf OR schedule OR dashboard)
      | eval lane="audit_report_lifecycle"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(audit_id, request_id, object_id, correlation_id, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, uid, resource_uid, "")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(actor, service_name, "grafana_audit"))))
      | eval render_ms=0
      | eval fail=if(match(lr,"failure|error|timeout|denied"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=if(match(lr,"schedule|report|pdf"), 1, 0)
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=if(scheduled_report==1, 1, 0)
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
| eval dash_key=if(len(trim(dashboard_uid))>0, trim(dashboard_uid), "unknown_uid")
| eval pod_name=coalesce(nullif(pod_name, ""), "unknown_pod")
| eval render_id=coalesce(nullif(render_id, ""), md5(dash_key.pod_name.toString(_time)))
| eval ms_for_org_p95=if(ctr_for_rate==1, render_ms, null())
| join type=left max=0 pod_name
    [| inputlookup grafana_renderer_inventory.csv
     | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(renderer_pod)))
     | fields pod_name owner team replicas memory_limit_mb supports_pdf canary_window ]
| eval renderer_owner=coalesce(owner, "unassigned")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(team, "platform_obs")
| eval replicas=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(replicas, "0"))), 10)
| eval mem_limit_mb=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(memory_limit_mb, "0"))), 10)
| eval supports_pdf=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(supports_pdf, "yes"))))
| eval canary_window_flag=if(match(lower(toString(canary_window)), "y|true|1|canary"), 1, 0)
| sort 0 dash_key _time
| streamstats time_window=15m window=500 count AS win_n sum(fail_for_rate) AS win_f sum(ctr_for_rate) AS win_c max(scheduled_report) AS sched_max BY dash_key
| eval fail_pct=if(win_c>0, round(100*win_f/nullif(win_c,0), 2), 0)
| streamstats time_window=15m window=500 perc95(render_ms) AS win_p95_ms BY dash_key
| eventstats perc95(ms_for_org_p95) AS org_p95_ms BY org_id
| eval severity=case(
    org_p95_ms>20000 AND fail_pct>10 AND sched_max>=1, "CRITICAL",
    fail_pct>5 AND win_n>=4, "HIGH",
    canary_window_flag==1 OR (lane=="tstats_perf_upstream" AND ds_latency_proxy>90), "MEDIUM",
    true(), "LOW")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL", "scale_renderer_raise_mem_shrink_heavy_dashboards_throttle_reports",
    severity=="HIGH", "inspect_hpa_chrome_flags_devshm_datasource_uid_correlation",
    severity=="MEDIUM", "review_canary_upstream_saturation_synthetic_probe_paths",
    true(), "monitor_continue_validate_inventory_lookup_freshness")
| table _time org_id dash_key render_id fail_pct win_p95_ms org_p95_ms severity sched_max renderer_owner owner_team replicas mem_limit_mb supports_pdf canary_window_flag recommended_action lane ds_latency_proxy datasource_uid pod_name
description: Grafana image rendering turns live dashboards into PNG and PDF artifacts through headless Chromium, either in a remote Grafana Image Renderer service or as a plugin and sidecar colocated with Grafana core. Executive pipelines and scheduled reports depend on that path: when Chromium exhausts memory, loses `/dev/shm`, or waits on slow datasources, `render_duration_ms` climbs, HTTP 500 and timeout strings appear, and navigation failed errors surface after DNS, TLS, or backoff events. Percentile tails hit reporting deadlines hard because PDF jobs are serialized and board decks batch many panels. Datasource UID correlation separates true renderer faults from upstream Prometheus federation lag, Loki query backpressure, or Mimir overload that inflates panel time before Chrome ever crashes. When pods receive OOMKilled signals, renderer capacity collapses and failure rates rise across dashboard_uid groups tied to the same pool. This use case differs from UC-13.6.1 service health and interactive load, UC-13.6.2 datasource query errors, UC-13.6.3 unified alerting evaluation duration, UC-13.6.4 plugin skew, UC-13.6.5 login audit, UC-13.6.6 annotation storms, UC-13.6.7 dashboard version revert, UC-13.6.8 API token monitoring, UC-13.6.10 permission audit, UC-13.6.11 LDAP team sync, and UC-13.6.13 notification delivery failures.
value: Platform and business intelligence leaders keep board packs, regulatory PDFs, and incident briefings on schedule because renderer health is explicit rather than guessed from angry chat messages. Operators see which dashboard_uid groups breach failure budgets, whether scheduled_report traffic triggered the surge, and which team owns remediation through inventory joins. FinOps receives evidence tying memory limits and replica counts to export success, supporting right-sizing without endless war rooms. Risk and audit stakeholders retain a tamper-resistant narrative correlating Grafana audit lifecycles, synthetic browser probes, Kubernetes resource signals, and log-derived Chromium errors. Customers of internal observability services experience fewer silent misses where Grafana UI looks fine yet automated exports failed for hours.
implementation: Land grafana:rendering and grafana:log renderer lines in index=observability, forward k8s_metrics with cadvisor pod metrics, ingest Splunk Synthetic checks for render URLs, accelerate CIM Performance for datasource hosts, and ship grafana:audit report events. Publish grafana_renderer_inventory.csv. Save observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate every fifteen minutes with earliest=-24h@h. Route CRITICAL and HIGH to platform and BI on-call. Archive weekly evidence exports.
visualization: Severity-colored table by dash_key and org_id with drilldowns to grafana:rendering, grafana:log, grafana:audit, synthetic browser rows, mstats pod series, and tstats Performance hosts; dual-axis trends for fail_pct and win_p95_ms; heatmap of renderer_owner by team.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t perc95(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS cpu_p95 latest(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS mem_used_pct FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
| rename Performance.host AS ds_host
| table ds_host cpu_p95 mem_used_pct
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Performance summaries for hosts and containers backing Prometheus, Loki, Mimir, Tempo, and CloudWatch datasources that feed heavily exported Grafana dashboards; retain at least thirty days for quarterly rendering resilience attestations paired with audit exports.
knownFalsePositives: Executive PDF surges during board preparation weeks legitimately raise concurrency and fail_pct without a defective renderer; require allow-list context and seasonal baselines before paging. A deliberately heavy synthetic dashboard with thirty or more panels can trip thresholds in labs; label those boards non-production or exclude them via lookup flags. Slow datasources unrelated to Chromium—Prometheus federation lag, Loki query frontend backpressure, or Mimir overload—inflate render time; use the Performance tstats arm and datasource_uid correlation before scaling renderer pods. Browser cache cold-start after pod replacement can produce one-off slow renders; demand sustained fifteen minute windows. Frontend feature flags that enable new charts may shift panel load abruptly; correlate change tickets. Custom panel plugins that lean on client-side rendering can make PDF exports look like renderer failures when the bottleneck is panel design. Public dashboard shares routed through reporting services may authenticate differently than interactive viewers; synthetic probes must mirror the real path. Locale-specific font fallback delays can present as intermittent navigation timeouts; validate multilingual PDF fixtures. Scheduled grafana-image-renderer horizontal pod autoscaler scale-up lag can appear as short bursts; inspect HPA events alongside fail_pct. Debug builds with heavier anti-aliasing inflate CPU and are not production baselines. Content security policy changes blocking external fonts or stylesheets can cascade into layout reflow timeouts mistaken for memory issues. NetData or duplicate cadvisor scrapers may double metric samples; dedupe before interpreting mstats. NetData or cAdvisor `/metrics` noise from non-renderer pods can pollute naive pod filters; tighten label selectors. Grafana datasource query timing logs may duplicate renderer errors when proxies retry; dedupe by render_id. Splunk Synthetic Monitoring maintenance windows can look like Grafana outages; annotate probe pauses. Lookup staleness after Kubernetes moves pods without updating grafana_renderer_inventory.csv causes false owner attribution, not false renderer failures.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — group episodes by grafana_instance and dashboard_uid when KPIs consume the same metrics indexes

detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with observability platform engineers who operate Grafana stacks, the business intelligence teams that publish executive PDFs, Kubernetes platform squads that schedule renderer pods, and site reliability engineers who guard rendering service levels for incident exports. This use case isolates the Grafana image rendering path: headless Chromium sessions that turn dashboards into PNG and PDF artifacts for scheduled reports, alert images, and manual exports. Architectures split into a remote rendering service that listens over HTTP for render jobs versus an in-process or sidecar plugin that launches Chrome adjacent to Grafana core. Remote mode reduces coupling but adds network hops and separate horizontal pod autoscaler policies; plugin mode shares fate with Grafana core yet simplifies service discovery. Shared memory sizing for Chromium maps directly to container `/dev/shm` limits and remains a frequent silent killer when defaults mirror generic microservice templates rather than browser guidance. Datasource UID correlation matters because renderer logs often echo the dashboard UID while backend query latency shows up under the datasource identifier; joining those identifiers prevents blaming the browser when Prometheus federation or Loki backpressure dominates wall clock. Scheduled report ownership lookups tie executive decks to accountable teams so paging routes to the right product owner instead of a generic platform queue. Chrome out-of-memory kills cascade: the container runtime signals OOMKilled, the renderer process disappears mid-navigation, Grafana surfaces HTTP five hundreds or client timeouts, and `render_duration_ms` tails stretch while failure counts climb. Failure signatures include explicit timeout strings, navigation failed messages after TLS or DNS blips, and five hundred responses when the renderer pool is saturated. This narrative differs from UC-13.6.1, which tracks interactive dashboard load and panel query duration for human viewers rather than export-time Chromium lifecycles. It differs from UC-13.6.2, which focuses on datasource query errors and blank panels rather than renderer health. UC-13.6.3 covers unified alerting evaluation duration, not PNG or PDF generation. UC-13.6.4 covers plugin version skew broadly, not renderer-specific Chromium crashes. UC-13.6.5 covers authentication auditing rather than export pipelines. UC-13.6.6 covers annotation storms. UC-13.6.7 covers dashboard version revert events. UC-13.6.8 covers API token lifecycle monitoring. UC-13.6.10 covers permission audits. UC-13.6.11 covers LDAP team synchronization. UC-13.6.13 covers notification delivery failures after alerts already fired. Splunk becomes the tamper-resistant ledger that fuses renderer container logs, Grafana core lines mentioning the image renderer plugin, headless Chromium stderr, Kubernetes pod metrics, optional synthetic browser probes hitting render URLs, Prometheus or NetData style scrape lines for cadvisor endpoints, and audit narratives for scheduled report lifecycles.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Stand up five complementary lanes the multisearch stanza fans in parallel, plus a lookup that inventories renderer pods. Lane one lands `sourcetype=grafana:rendering` when you operate the standalone Grafana Image Renderer service, merged with `sourcetype=grafana:log` lines that mention renderer, image-renderer, Chromium, or headless namespaces inside Grafana core. Normalize `render_id`, `correlation_id`, `request_id`, and `trace_id` into a single join key using coalesce after ingestion parsers stabilize. Preserve `dashboard_uid`, `datasource_uid`, `org_id`, `render_duration_ms`, HTTP status, and pod name fields emitted by OpenTelemetry collectors or universal forwarders tailing container stdout. Lane two queries `mstats` against `index=k8s_metrics` for `container_cpu_usage_seconds_total` and `container_memory_working_set_bytes` grouped by pod labels that match your renderer naming convention. The arm filters pod names containing render, image, or grafana tokens so unrelated workloads do not inflate the join. Analysts correlate spikes in working set bytes with Chrome tab lifetimes and PDF rasterization bursts. Lane three ingests Splunk Synthetic Monitoring or equivalent `index=synthetic` browser checks that hit the HTTPS render endpoint or a dashboard deep link used by reporting jobs. Failures there isolate edge TLS, cookie, and content-security-policy issues that logs alone under-report. Lane four runs `tstats` against the accelerated Splunk CIM Performance data model, grouping by `Performance.host` for datasource and query backend machines that feed the dashboards being exported. Elevated `cpu_load_percent` tails act as a RED-style saturation proxy when datasource query durations are not directly modeled, explaining renderer timeouts that are actually upstream slowness. Lane five searches `index=grafana_audit` or observability-indexed `grafana:audit` for report scheduling, render, PDF, and dashboard lifecycle verbs so scheduled job bursts align with Chromium load. Publish `lookups/grafana_renderer_inventory.csv` with columns `renderer_pod`, `owner`, `team`, `replicas`, `memory_limit_mb`, `supports_pdf`, and `canary_window` refreshed whenever Kubernetes deployments change. Grafana provisioning files should declare renderer URL, authentication headers, and concurrency limits consistent with vendor guidance. Horizontal pod autoscaler rules belong in Git with explicit minimum replicas during business hours when executive PDFs run. OpenTelemetry collector pipelines should scrape cadvisor or kubelet summary metrics into `k8s_metrics` with pod label enrichment. Synthetic probes should use the same authentication path as scheduled reports, not an overly privileged service account that hides real user failures. Allow-list dashboards that qualify for rendering service level objectives so FinOps reviews do not chase lab boards.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate with a fifteen minute schedule during steady production, dispatch earliest=-24h@h, dispatch latest=now, and alert when severity is CRITICAL or HIGH. Throttle duplicate dash_key rows for thirty minutes unless severity escalates. Include recommended_action, fail_pct, and win_p95_ms fragments in pager narratives so incident commanders open renderer runbooks without rerunning ad hoc searches.

Pipeline narrative: multisearch fans renderer logs, Kubernetes mstats for renderer pods, synthetic browser results for render URLs, Performance model summaries for datasource host pressure, and Grafana audit lines for scheduled report lifecycles. After union, coalesce builds a stable render_id while ms_for_org_p95 restricts organization percentiles to real render attempts only. The join to grafana_renderer_inventory.csv enriches owner, team, replica counts, memory limits, PDF capability flags, and canary windows. streamstats with time_window=15m computes trailing failure_rate per dashboard_uid via dash_key using summed fail_for_rate over summed ctr_for_rate in the trailing window, while a second streamstats pass estimates trailing render duration percentiles. eventstats adds org-wide p95 baselines for render_ms on qualifying events. case maps CRITICAL when organization p95 exceeds twenty seconds, fail_pct exceeds ten percent, and scheduled_report activity appeared in the window; HIGH when fail_pct exceeds five percent with sufficient sample depth; MEDIUM when canary_window flags or upstream Performance pressure dominates; LOW otherwise. recommended_action encodes scale-up, Chrome flag, shared memory, and datasource correlation steps without pretending Splunk replaces kubectl.

Fenced SPL must match the top-level spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.6.9 Grafana image renderer timeout failure rate multisearch renderer logs k8s_metrics synthetic tstats Performance audit join grafana_renderer_inventory earliest=-24h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=observability (sourcetype="grafana:rendering" OR (sourcetype="grafana:log" (renderer OR "image-renderer" OR rendering OR chromium OR headless))) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval lane="renderer_core_log"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(render_id, correlation_id, request_id, trace_id, x_request_id, renderId, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, dashboardUid, uid, dash_uid, "")))
      | eval datasource_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(datasource_uid, datasourceUid, ds_uid, "")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pod_name, pod, k8s_pod_name, kube_pod, ""))))
      | eval render_ms=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(render_duration_ms, duration_ms, elapsed_ms, "0"))), 10)
      | eval http_st=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(http_status, status_code, "200"))), 10)
      | eval fail=if(http_st>=500 OR match(lr,"timeout|timed out|navigation failed|chrome crashed|oomkilled|killed process|context deadline|econnreset"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=if(match(lr,"scheduled report|reporting|pdf|png export|render_key|report job"), 1, 0)
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=1
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
    [ | mstats avg(_value) AS metric_val WHERE index=k8s_metrics earliest=-24h@h latest=now span=5m (metric_name="container_cpu_usage_seconds_total" OR metric_name="container_memory_working_set_bytes") BY pod, metric_name
      | eval lane="k8s_mstats_renderer"
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(pod)))
      | where like(pod_name,"%render%") OR like(pod_name,"%image%") OR like(pod_name,"%grafana%")
      | eval org_id="0"
      | eval dashboard_uid="k8s_metrics_join"
      | eval render_id=strcat("mstats_", pod_name, "_", replace(metric_name, ".", "_"))
      | eval render_ms=if(metric_name=="container_cpu_usage_seconds_total", round(metric_val*1000,2), round(metric_val/1048576,4))
      | eval fail=if(metric_name=="container_memory_working_set_bytes" AND metric_val>1073741824 AND like(pod_name,"%render%"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=0
      | eval ctr_for_rate=0
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=round(metric_val,6) ]
    [ search index=synthetic sourcetype=synthetic:browser earliest=-24h@h latest=now (url=*render* OR url=*grafana*)
      | eval lane="synthetic_browser_render"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(check_id, test_id, run_id, correlation_id, synthetic_id, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, dashboardUid, target_uid, "synthetic_board")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name="synthetic_probe"
      | eval render_ms=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(duration_ms, response_time_ms, ttfb_ms, "0"))), 10)
      | eval http_st=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(http_status, status_code, "200"))), 10)
      | eval fail=if(http_st>=500 OR match(lr,"timeout|error|failed navigation"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=1
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
    [ | tstats summariesonly=t perc95(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS ds_latency_proxy FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
      | rename Performance.host AS perf_host
      | eval lane="tstats_perf_upstream"
      | eval perf_host=lower(trim(toString(perf_host)))
      | eval dashboard_uid="upstream_datasource_pressure"
      | eval org_id="0"
      | eval pod_name=perf_host
      | eval render_id=strcat("perf_", perf_host)
      | eval render_ms=0
      | eval fail=if(ds_latency_proxy>92, 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=0
      | eval fail_for_rate=0
      | eval ctr_for_rate=0
      | eval datasource_uid=perf_host ]
    [ search (index=grafana_audit OR index=observability) sourcetype="grafana:audit" earliest=-24h@h latest=now (report OR render OR pdf OR schedule OR dashboard)
      | eval lane="audit_report_lifecycle"
      | eval lr=lower(_raw)
      | eval render_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(audit_id, request_id, object_id, correlation_id, ""))))
      | eval dashboard_uid=trim(toString(coalesce(dashboard_uid, uid, resource_uid, "")))
      | eval org_id=trim(toString(coalesce(org_id, orgId, "0")))
      | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(actor, service_name, "grafana_audit"))))
      | eval render_ms=0
      | eval fail=if(match(lr,"failure|error|timeout|denied"), 1, 0)
      | eval scheduled_report=if(match(lr,"schedule|report|pdf"), 1, 0)
      | eval fail_for_rate=fail
      | eval ctr_for_rate=if(scheduled_report==1, 1, 0)
      | eval ds_latency_proxy=0 ]
| eval dash_key=if(len(trim(dashboard_uid))>0, trim(dashboard_uid), "unknown_uid")
| eval pod_name=coalesce(nullif(pod_name, ""), "unknown_pod")
| eval render_id=coalesce(nullif(render_id, ""), md5(strcat(dash_key, pod_name, toString(_time))))
| eval ms_for_org_p95=if(ctr_for_rate==1, render_ms, null())
| join type=left max=0 pod_name
    [| inputlookup grafana_renderer_inventory.csv
     | eval pod_name=lower(trim(toString(renderer_pod)))
     | fields pod_name owner team replicas memory_limit_mb supports_pdf canary_window ]
| eval renderer_owner=coalesce(owner, "unassigned")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(team, "platform_obs")
| eval replicas=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(replicas, "0"))), 10)
| eval mem_limit_mb=tonumber(trim(toString(coalesce(memory_limit_mb, "0"))), 10)
| eval supports_pdf=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(supports_pdf, "yes"))))
| eval canary_window_flag=if(match(lower(toString(canary_window)), "y|true|1|canary"), 1, 0)
| sort 0 dash_key _time
| streamstats time_window=15m window=500 count AS win_n sum(fail_for_rate) AS win_f sum(ctr_for_rate) AS win_c max(scheduled_report) AS sched_max BY dash_key
| eval fail_pct=if(win_c>0, round(100*win_f/nullif(win_c,0), 2), 0)
| streamstats time_window=15m window=500 perc95(render_ms) AS win_p95_ms BY dash_key
| eventstats perc95(ms_for_org_p95) AS org_p95_ms BY org_id
| eval severity=case(
    org_p95_ms>20000 AND fail_pct>10 AND sched_max>=1, "CRITICAL",
    fail_pct>5 AND win_n>=4, "HIGH",
    canary_window_flag==1 OR (lane=="tstats_perf_upstream" AND ds_latency_proxy>90), "MEDIUM",
    true(), "LOW")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL", "scale_renderer_raise_mem_shrink_heavy_dashboards_throttle_reports",
    severity=="HIGH", "inspect_hpa_chrome_flags_devshm_datasource_uid_correlation",
    severity=="MEDIUM", "review_canary_upstream_saturation_synthetic_probe_paths",
    true(), "monitor_continue_validate_inventory_lookup_freshness")
| table _time org_id dash_key render_id fail_pct win_p95_ms org_p95_ms severity sched_max renderer_owner owner_team replicas mem_limit_mb supports_pdf canary_window_flag recommended_action lane ds_latency_proxy datasource_uid pod_name
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate_alert]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
action.email = 1
action.email.to = platform-renderer-oncall@example.com
action.email.subject = Grafana renderer $result.severity$ dash=$result.dash_key$
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch observability_uc_13_6_9_grafana_image_renderer_fail_rate | where severity IN ("CRITICAL","HIGH")
```

Performance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queue time above internal targets, materialize each lane hourly into a summary index keyed on dash_key and pod_name, widen detection windows on summaries, and retain this full search for investigations.

Step 4 — Validate

Ground truth begins inside Kubernetes: kubectl describe pod on a renderer pod should show memory limits, `/dev/shm` size, restart reasons, and OOMKilled events that align with Splunk log narratives for the same minute. Compare Grafana reporting UI job history with audit rows to ensure scheduled jobs are not failing silently due to authentication.

Positive validation in lab: import or construct a synthetic dashboard with at least thirty heavy time-series panels, assign it a stable dashboard_uid on the allow-list, and trigger repeated PDF exports while observing renderer logs for rising `render_duration_ms`. Throttle CPU on the renderer pod using cgroup limits or Kubernetes CPU limits under change control, confirm the search raises HIGH or CRITICAL with non-null recommended_action, then restore CPU and confirm severity returns to LOW within two windows.

Negative validation: restore healthy CPU and memory, ensure `/dev/shm` is sized per vendor guidance, and confirm the alert wrapper emits zero rows when filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH only for thirty consecutive runs on a quiet tenant.

Intentional `/dev/shm` regression: temporarily shrink shared memory in a non-production namespace only, repeat a PDF export, and confirm Chromium crash signatures move Splunk fail_pct upward with recommended_action pointing at shared memory and Chrome flags before reverting the manifest.

Cross-check synthetic probes: when Splunk Synthetic Monitoring reports failures, open the raw HAR or step log for the same interval and verify TLS, redirect, and cookie behavior matches Grafana audit expectations.

Stakeholder review: BI leadership should agree that paging thresholds align with board-meeting calendars and that allow-listed dashboards cover executive decks.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case A — Renderer pod OOMKilled silently due to low memory limit: inspect termination reason and last state, raise memory requests and limits under FinOps guardrails, verify PDF rasterization no longer spikes working set beyond limit, and re-run export smoke tests.

Case B — Datasource UID join missing because Grafana dropped User-Agent or custom headers in access logs: re-enable structured logging for renderer requests, confirm parsers retain datasource_uid, and replay a failing export while tailing logs to validate field presence.

Case C — Horizontal pod autoscaler stuck because CPU metrics unavailable: repair metrics-server or kubelet summary scrape paths, confirm k8s_metrics ingests container_cpu_usage_seconds_total for renderer pods, and validate HPA status conditions before blaming Chromium.

Case D — Scheduled report queue backlog from Grafana job throttling: widen concurrency carefully, stagger cron schedules, deduplicate duplicate report definitions, and coordinate with BI owners to avoid duplicate PDFs to the same distribution list.

Case E — Custom panel plugin renders client-side and skews fail metrics: identify panels that defer work to the browser, exclude them from PDF targets or snapshot static images, and document exceptions in the allow-list metadata.

Case F — Shared `/dev/shm` exhausted by sibling renderer on the same node: cordon or spread pods via anti-affinity, increase shm per pod, or split remote renderer pools so noisy tenants do not starve executive jobs.

Case G — TLS handshake to internal datasource failing: validate corporate CA bundles inside renderer image, check SNI and cipher compatibility, and compare Grafana datasource test from core versus renderer network paths.

Case H — Synthetic browser test uses stale cookie causing auth redirect loops: rotate probe credentials, align login flow with multi-factor policies, and pin probes to service accounts approved for headless use.

Case I — Locale font missing causing layout reflow timeout: bake font packages into renderer image, verify locale packs in container layers, and re-test PDFs for non-English org units.

Case J — HTTP Event Collector backpressure drops renderer logs: scale ingest tier, widen token rate limits, and add a local file buffer on collectors until loss stops; mute alerts only after verifying zero drops.

Case K — Dashboard with very long native time range causes PDF export timeout: shorten default range for reporting snapshots, use report time override features, or precompute weekly aggregates to keep Chromium within navigation budgets.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists five arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, join, inputlookup, mstats, and tstats appear; final table lists eighteen operational columns.
Teaching notes for junior engineers: renderer failures are multi-modal. Logs speak to Chromium and navigation, mstats speak to CPU and RAM envelopes, synthetic probes speak to edge auth and TLS, Performance tstats speak to datasource hosts, and audit speaks to who scheduled what. Combine lanes before declaring a single root cause.

Risk briefing: executive board weeks legitimately multiply PDF concurrency; tune allow-lists and seasonal thresholds rather than muting the detection outright. Deliberately heavy synthetic dashboards with thirty or more panels are valid stress fixtures when labeled non-production.

Collector posture: stamp change_ticket_id onto forwarded events when renderer images, Chrome major versions, or Grafana provisioning changes land. When log volume is high, route renderer sourcetypes to hot retention indexes with fast search acceleration for investigations.

FinOps alignment: memory increases for Chromium have real cost; pair alerts with monthly summaries of fail_pct by owner_team to justify capacity.

Security alignment: renderer logs can contain dashboard titles and datasource UIDs; minimize raw query text in evidence packs when policy requires.

Compliance alignment: scheduled report history supports operational resilience narratives; archive weekly CSV exports filtered to CRITICAL and HIGH severities with ticket references.

Runbook integration: link Kubernetes OOMKilled troubleshooting, Grafana reporting documentation, and Splunk Lantern onboarding articles in the same portal page as this saved search.

Continuous improvement: after incidents, capture before-and-after win_p95_ms distributions to prove return to steady state.

Documentation: when renaming renderer deployments, update grafana_renderer_inventory.csv the same day to avoid stale joins.

Governance: require peer review for dashboards added to the executive allow-list so rendering SLOs stay meaningful.

Stakeholder communication: translate severity rows into customer-visible impact using owner_team and mem_limit_mb context.

Tooling: optional Splunk ITSI modules may group episodes by grafana_instance entities when KPIs consume the same metrics indexes.

Post-incident prompts: ask whether a Chrome upgrade, an HPA max replica change, or a datasource saturation event coincided with the window before closing tickets.

Vendor support bundles should include redacted renderer Deployment YAML, HPA describe output, and Splunk search results for the alert window.

Edge cases include IPv6-only clients and split-horizon DNS that affect remote renderer mode differently than plugin mode.

Rate limits at reverse proxies can return five hundreds without Chromium errors; correlate proxy logs alongside renderer logs.

Cold starts after node drains can spike fail_pct until caches warm; pair with Kubernetes maintenance timelines.

Training drills should rehearse interpreting multisearch rows when only synthetic lane moves versus only renderer log lane.

Long-term improvement: promote recording rules for queries that repeatedly appear as datasource pressure in tstats rows.

Appendix note: pair with UC-13.6.2 when panels error rather than merely time out in the renderer.

Appendix note: pair with UC-13.6.1 when interactive load is slow but exports remain healthy.

Appendix note: document Grafana feature toggles that affect reporting concurrency so validators interpret regressions after toggle flips.

Appendix note: duplicate scrapers of kubelet metrics can inflate mstats samples; dedupe architecturally before raising thresholds.

Appendix note: legal reviewers may require minimization of dashboard titles in evidence packs; store hashes instead when policy demands.

Appendix note: teach executives that seasonal reporting spikes are expected; use sched_max reasoning before declaring incidents.

Appendix note: PNG alert images share renderer pools with PDF jobs; capacity planning must count both workloads.

Appendix note: OpenTelemetry exemplars can augment traces from renderer to datasource when tracing is enabled end to end.

Appendix note: summarize monthly counts of CRITICAL breaches by owner_team for capacity reviews.

Appendix note: integrate maintenance metadata from change systems to annotate known Chrome upgrades only with written approval.

Appendix note: for hybrid estates, ensure cloud and on-prem Grafana instances use distinct org_id labels to avoid accidental merges.

Appendix note: revisit fail_pct thresholds quarterly as median dashboard complexity grows fleet-wide.

Appendix note: Windows-hosted Grafana with remote renderer on Linux remains supported; document path separators in provisioning files.

Appendix note: mainframe bridges are out of scope unless Prometheus exporters front those systems.

Appendix note: when Tempo or Loki queries dominate panel time, renderer timeouts may reflect query skew rather than Chromium alone.

Appendix note: public dashboard shares via reporting services can bypass standard auth; validate synthetic probes mirror the public path when applicable.

Appendix note: debug builds with heavier anti-aliasing inflate CPU; segregate debug environments from production renderer pools.

Appendix note: Content Security Policy changes can block external fonts and cascade into layout timeouts; correlate CSP deploy timelines with fail_pct.

Appendix note: Prometheus federation slowness often elevates datasource wall clock before renderer logs show navigation failures.

Appendix note: Loki backpressure elevates query duration and should be diagnosed on query frontend logs, not only on Chromium stderr.

Appendix note: browser cache cold-start after pod replacement can cause one-off slow renders; require sustained windows before paging.

Appendix note: frontend feature flags that enable new charts can shift panel load abruptly; annotate flag timelines in change records.

Appendix note: scheduled grafana-image-renderer HPA scale-up lag may present as short bursts; use sched_max and win_n depth before CRITICAL.

Appendix note: locale-specific font fallback delays present as intermittent navigation timeouts; test multi-locale PDFs monthly.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the picture-making service that turns live charts into files for meetings and slide decks. When it chokes, the pictures never arrive and important reviews slip. We raise a clear signal before the room goes quiet waiting on a blank screen.
hardware: Applies to Grafana OSS, Grafana Enterprise, and Grafana Cloud connected estates running the Grafana Image Renderer as a remote service or sidecar on Linux containers in Kubernetes, with optional synthetic probes and Prometheus-compatible metric stores.
